Row1.Pathway ID/Name: HER2 signaling / ERBB2 activation – KEGG: ErbB signaling
Row1.Rationale: Activating HER2 mutations and amplification in NSCLC create oncogene addiction; T‑DM1 exploits this by binding HER2, blocking signaling, and delivering DM1, with clinical responses observed particularly in HER2‑mutant and strongly amplified tumors.

Row2.Rationale: HER2/HER3 dimers signal through PI3K–AKT–mTOR; PTEN loss or PIK3CA alterations can sustain this pathway despite HER2 blockade, reducing apoptosis after DM1 exposure and contributing to primary or acquired resistance to T‑DM1.

Row3.Rationale: Parallel activation of MAPK via KRAS/BRAF/MAP2K1 mutations or other RTKs maintains proliferation even when HER2 is targeted, decreasing dependence on HER2 and limiting the impact of T‑DM1 in HER2‑altered NSCLC.

Row4.Rationale: ERBB family and bypass RTKs can activate JAK/STAT (e.g., STAT3); persistent STAT signaling provides survival cues under upstream inhibition and is implicated in reduced sensitivity to HER2‑directed therapies, including ADCs.

Row5.Pathway ID/Name: Cell cycle G2/M checkpoint – Reactome: R-HSA-69275
Row5.Rationale: DM1-induced microtubule disruption normally triggers G2/M arrest and mitotic catastrophe; impaired cyclin B1/CDK1 activation or altered mitotic regulators allows escape from mitotic death and has been associated with resistance to T‑DM1 and other microtubule‑targeting agents.

Row6.Pathway ID/Name: Microtubule dynamics and mitotic spindle checkpoint – Reactome: R-HSA-69206
Row6.Rationale: When spindle‑assembly and DNA‑damage checkpoints are intact, DM1 binding to tubulin leads to sustained mitotic arrest and apoptosis, consistent with observed responses and disease control in HER2‑mutant NSCLC treated with T‑DM1.

Row7.Rationale: Overexpression of anti‑apoptotic proteins (BCL‑2, BCL‑XL) dampens mitochondrial apoptosis after DM1‑induced damage; ADC studies show that such shifts in BCL‑2 family balance confer payload resistance, likely diminishing T‑DM1‑induced cell death in lung cancer.

Row8.Pathway ID/Name: Endocytosis and early endosome trafficking – RAB‑regulated – Reactome: R-HSA-8856828
Row8.Rationale: T‑DM1 efficacy depends on HER2‑mediated endocytosis and routing to lysosomes; efficient RAB‑regulated endocytic trafficking in HER2‑mutant NSCLC promotes adequate intracellular DM1 delivery and is linked to better clinical responses.

Row9.Pathway ID/Name: Lysosomal processing and payload release – Reactome: R-HSA-1236975
Row9.Rationale: With its non‑cleavable linker, T‑DM1 requires lysosomal proteolysis and acidic pH to generate lysine‑MCC‑DM1; impaired V‑ATPase activity, altered lysosomal pH, or loss of SLC46A3 reduce cytosolic DM1, a recognized ADC resistance mechanism relevant to HER2‑altered NSCLC.

Row10.Pathway ID/Name: Drug efflux and ABC transporters (MDR1/ABCB1, ABCCs, ABCG2) – KEGG: hsa02010
Row10.Rationale: DM1 catabolites are substrates for multiple ABC transporters; overexpression of MDR1/ABCB1, ABCC2, or ABCG2 lowers intracellular DM1 exposure and has been associated with reduced T‑DM1 activity and cross‑resistance to other microtubule inhibitors.

Row11.Pathway ID/Name: Bypass RTK signaling (HER3, MET and others) – KEGG: hsa04012/hsa04013
Row11.Rationale: Activation of HER3 (via NRG1), MET or other RTKs sustains PI3K–AKT and MAPK signaling despite HER2 blockade; experimental HER2‑targeted therapy data and NSCLC resistance patterns indicate that such bypass networks undermine T‑DM1 efficacy.

Row12.Rationale: EMT and AXL upregulation in NSCLC promote invasive behavior and broad drug tolerance via MAPK, PI3K/AKT, and NF‑κB activation and suppression of pro‑apoptotic BIM; this plastic, mesenchymal state is linked to resistance to targeted agents and is expected to attenuate T‑DM1 responses in HER2‑altered tumors.
